events.name: litellm-acompletion
events.input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools.   "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "degraded",
    "status_justification": [
      "All 16 pods in namespace sock-shop are currently Running and Ready; no pods are Pending, CrashLooping, or Failed.",
      "Events show transient startup probe failures for carts, but the pod is now healthy, indicating recent but resolved readiness issues.",
      "At least one non-business pod (flash-agent) with restarts is present, suggesting external/chaos tooling activity in the namespace.",
      "Resource metrics APIs (metrics.k8s.io and Prometheus tools) are unavailable to this MCP user, so live resource saturation cannot be ruled out."
    ],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "partial",
      "gaps": [
        "metrics_unavailable",
        "events_truncated"
      ],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score slightly reduced due to missing live CPU/memory/network metrics and incomplete event history."
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [
      "Pod inventory for sock-shop shows 16 pods, all in Running status with full readiness (READY 1/1 or 2/2).",
      "Core microservices (carts, carts-db, catalogue, catalogue-db, front-end, orders, orders-db, payment, queue-master, rabbitmq, shipping, user, user-db) are all present and Running.",
      "Two observability/control-plane pods exist in the namespace: kubernetes-mcp-server and prometheus-mcp-server, both Running.",
      "A flash-agent pod (flash-agent-768c8f4599-cphrf) is present with 2/2 containers and 3 restarts, which is atypical for the sock-shop app and likely represents an external agent or chaos/flash tool.",
      "Events for carts show Unhealthy warnings due to startup probe failures (timeouts and connection refused) shortly after scheduling, but no ongoing failures are reported.",
      "No events mentioning Inject, Kill, Chaos, Disrupt, or Fault are visible in the truncated event output, but the presence of flash-agent strongly suggests external disturbance tooling.",
      "The pods_top call against metrics.k8s.io is forbidden for this service account, so per-pod CPU/memory usage cannot be retrieved.",
      "Prometheus-related tools (list_metrics, execute_query) are not available in this MCP environment despite the presence of a prometheus-mcp-server pod."
    ],
    "analysis_approach": [
      "Listed all pods scoped to namespace sock-shop to establish fleet composition and pod health.",
      "Reviewed pod phases and READY columns to determine immediate health and availability.",
      "Fetched namespace-scoped events to identify warnings, errors, and any signs of probe failures or scheduling issues.",
      "Attempted to gather live resource metrics via pods_top (metrics.k8s.io) and, when that failed, via Prometheus-style tools; both paths were unavailable, so resource analysis is limited.",
      "Looked for signs of chaos or external disturbance by identifying unusual pods (flash-agent) and scanning event reasons (though truncated) for disturbance-related keywords.",
      "Assessed restarts and non-standard components to distinguish between intrinsic application faults and externally induced disturbances."
    ],
    "observability_gaps": [
      "functions.pods_top: forbidden for pods.metrics.k8s.io in namespace sock-shop for this service account.",
      "Prometheus tools (list_metrics, execute_query) reported as unknown tools by the MCP server, despite a prometheus-mcp-server pod existing.",
      "events_list output is truncated, so later or less common events (including potential chaos-related events) may not be visible.",
      "No direct access to pod descriptions (kubectl describe equivalent) was used in this run, so detailed probe configurations and container statuses are inferred from events only."
    ]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 16,
    "healthy_pods": 16,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 1,
    "overall_health_score": 85
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "warning",
      "component": "carts-6b5d88f967-dj7f5",
      "category": "application-probes",
      "summary": "Carts pod experienced transient startup probe failures (timeouts and connection refused on /health) shortly after deployment, but is now Running and Ready.",
      "recommended_action": "Review the carts service startup probe configuration (timeoutSeconds, periodSeconds, failureThreshold) and application startup time. If the application is slow to start under normal conditions, relax the startup probe thresholds to avoid unnecessary Unhealthy events; if not, inspect carts logs around startup for slow initialization or dependency delays."
    },
    {
      "severity": "info",
      "component": "flash-agent-768c8f4599-cphrf",
      "category": "external-disturbance",
      "summary": "Flash-agent pod with 2 containers and 3 restarts is running in the sock-shop namespace, indicating an external agent or disturbance/chaos tooling is present.",
      "recommended_action": "Treat this as an external/management component rather than an application fault. Confirm with the owning team whether flash-agent is intentionally deployed for chaos, testing, or security purposes. Monitor for any namespace-wide disruptions correlated with this agent rather than deleting or scaling it from within this analysis scope."
    },
    {
      "severity": "info",
      "component": "observability",
      "category": "monitoring",
      "summary": "Resource metrics for pods in sock-shop are not accessible: metrics.k8s.io is forbidden for this service account, and Prometheus query tools are not available.",
      "recommended_action": "Ensure the MCP service account has RBAC permissions to read pods.metrics.k8s.io in namespace sock-shop, or deploy/enable a metrics-server with appropriate RBAC. Additionally, expose Prometheus query APIs to the MCP server (or configure the existing prometheus-mcp-server) so that per-pod CPU, memory, and network metrics can be queried for more accurate health assessments."
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "Namespace sock-shop is currently in a generally healthy state with all 16 pods Running and Ready. The core microservices and their backing databases are up, and there are no active pod failures. However, there is evidence of transient startup probe issues for the carts service and the presence of an external flash-agent pod with restarts, suggesting that external tooling may be interacting with the namespace. Observability is limited by lack of access to live resource metrics.",
    "concerns": [
      "Transient startup probe failures for the carts service could indicate tight probe thresholds or occasional slow startup, which may cause noisy alerts or, under load, potential availability blips.",
      "The flash-agent pod, while likely intentional, introduces an external factor that could affect workload behavior; its restarts may correlate with disturbance or test scenarios.",
      "Lack of access to metrics.k8s.io and Prometheus metrics prevents verification of resource headroom and may hide early signs of CPU/memory pressure or network issues."
    ],
    "recommendations": [
      "Probe tuning for carts: Inspect the carts deployment configuration to confirm startupProbe settings and compare them with actual application initialization time. Adjust thresholds if necessary to reduce false Unhealthy events.",
      "Log review for carts and other core services: Even though pods are Running, review recent logs for carts, front-end, and user services to ensure there are no intermittent errors or dependency timeouts that do not surface as pod-level failures.",
      "Clarify flash-agent purpose: Coordinate with the team that deployed flash-agent to understand its role (chaos testing, security scanning, etc.) and ensure its behavior and restart patterns are expected and documented.",
      "Namespace hygiene: Confirm that non-application components (flash-agent, kubernetes-mcp-server, prometheus-mcp-server) are intentionally colocated in sock-shop and that their resource requests/limits are set to avoid contention with business workloads."
    ],
    "observability_recommendations": [
      "Grant the MCP service account (system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server) read access to pods.metrics.k8s.io in namespace sock-shop so that pods_top can return CPU and memory usage.",
      "Integrate Prometheus with MCP: configure or expose a Prometheus query endpoint and corresponding MCP tools (e.g., execute_query) so that per-pod metrics and potential chaos-related metrics can be queried.",
      "Expand event retention or retrieval: ensure events_list can return full, untruncated event history for the namespace to better diagnose intermittent issues and detect chaos-related events.",
      "Optionally deploy or configure additional in-namespace monitoring (e.g., sidecar or lightweight agent) if cluster-level metrics access cannot be granted, to provide at least basic CPU/memory telemetry for sock-shop pods."
    ]
  }
